data.title: The divergence in value logic between Eastern and Western Crypto KOLs
data.description:
Author: Crypto Veda Source: X, @thecryptoskanda

"Chinese Crypto Twitter lacks KOLs. In the English-speaking world, at least there are voices representing institutional perspectives like @hosseeb, @KyleSamani, and @cdixon. The Chinese-speaking world is entirely comprised of independent media—this situation needs to change, otherwise Chinese crypto will continue to decline."

I might have a little bit of authority to speak on this matter.

Because of my work with @Hertzflow_xyz, I frequently come into contact with various VCs, both in the East and West.

Once you've interacted with them, you'll notice a striking difference: The East cares about the specifics of how your project will generate revenue—tactics, token issuance, business model, logistics logic—treating it like a business transaction. The West, on the other hand, focuses on the big picture, on a story that can be sold at 10x or 100x the return. This leads to completely opposite definitions of "key opinion" between East and West. The Eastern logic is clearly about deconstructing everything; I, in my humble opinion, am perhaps the most typical Eastern KOL. The Western logic is about standing on the shoulders of giants—solving a well-known bottleneck in a highly valued or profitable industry using technology and a viable business model. (As Teacher Nie Xiao said, "Those who betray me get 1 million, those who are loyal to me get hundreds of millions—it's all just a matter of laughter." Applause, please.) The core of Western narratives lies in the fact that even if you know the details of these narratives are untenable, it doesn't matter. Even if those stories can't be thoroughly investigated, it doesn't matter; don't say anything detrimental to unity. Because they know that the true core of this narrative logic is to first come up with a story to lure key capital into a deal—then use the "unlimited funding method" to launch a saturation attack on potential obstacles and competitors, and win. Therefore, this is why the biggest narratives, the strongest capital deals, and the most sophisticated stories all come from North America and are told through the mouths of Western VCs: such as high-performance L1, Rerererererestaking, Rollup, FHE, Hyperliquid, etc. However, the vast majority of the industry's largest and most profitable businesses are basically in the hands of the Eastern camp: CEXs, payments, DEXs (Pancake/Raydium), aggregators (Jupiter)... Behind this is partly the issue of funding costs (North American institutions' financing costs are close to zero because of the passive support from pension funds and other entities due to government coercion). But more importantly, the social environment in Europe and America is still too privileged. Easterners have witnessed the best narratives and the most powerful products ultimately leading to a million "unnatural deaths." Our social and cultural deconstruction is naturally more PVP (Power, Responsibility, and Ability) than theirs. Of course, they are now also looking towards the East, and the next generation will follow the East's path, albeit in a different way. Therefore, I slightly disagree with the following "degeneration theory"—the East is simply different, and in a completely opposite way. Only when opinions have a fundamental impact on others' thinking can they be considered successful. Does this mean that Eastern KOLs are only successful when they challenge Western narrative values, which are considered "less hold up to scrutiny," and replace them with our own unique "value logic"? "Since we can't be more like them than they are, let them try to prove to us in their fear that they understand us better than we understand them."

data.title: BTC Valuation Report for Q1 2026: Target Price $185,500
data.description:
Authors: Daniel Kim, Ryan Yoon Source: Tiger Research Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

This report, written by Tiger Research, presents our outlook for Bitcoin in the first quarter of 2026, with a target price of $185,500.

Key Takeaways

  1. A solid macro environment, with a slowdown in the upward momentum: The Fed's interest rate cuts and M2 money supply growth are progressing steadily, but $4.57 billion in ETF outflows have impacted short-term trends. The CLARITY Act is expected to attract large banks.

Macroeconomic easing continues, market rally weakens

Bitcoin is currently trading around $96,000, down 12% from the previous report published on October 23, 2025. Nevertheless, the macroeconomic environment surrounding Bitcoin remains robust.

The Federal Reserve continues its accommodative policy stance. Between September and December 2025, the Fed cut interest rates three times, each time by 25 basis points, maintaining the current benchmark interest rate in the range of 3.50% to 3.75%. The dot plot released in December shows that interest rates are expected to fall to 3.4% by the end of 2026. The likelihood of a significant rate cut of 50 basis points or more this year is low. However, Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell's term will end this May, and the Trump administration may appoint a more dovish successor, which will ensure the continuation of the loose monetary policy trend. Despite the shift towards a looser macroeconomic environment, institutional investors' willingness to enter the market has cooled. The spot Bitcoin ETF recorded $4.57 billion in outflows in November and December 2025, marking the largest single-month outflow since the product's inception. Net capital inflows for the year totaled $21.4 billion, a 39% decrease from $35.2 billion last year. Asset rebalancing in January boosted inflows, but the sustainability of this rebound remains to be seen. Meanwhile, companies such as MicroStrategy (holding 673,783 Bitcoins, representing 3.2% of the circulating supply), Metaplanet, and Mara continued to increase their Bitcoin holdings. The CLARITY Act as a Policy Catalyst: Against the backdrop of stagnant institutional demand, the advancement of regulatory policies has become a potential market driver. The CLARITY Act, passed by the U.S. House of Representatives, clarifies the division of jurisdiction between the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), while allowing banks to provide digital asset custody and staking services. The act also grants the CFTC regulatory authority over the digital commodity spot market, providing a clear legal framework for the compliant operation of exchanges and brokers. The U.S. Senate Banking Committee is scheduled to review the bill on January 15. If the bill passes, it is expected to attract previously hesitant traditional financial institutions to enter the market. Ample Market Liquidity, Bitcoin Lagging Performance Besides regulatory progress, liquidity is another key variable affecting the market. Global M2 money supply reached a record high in the fourth quarter of 2024 and is still on an upward trend. Historically, Bitcoin price movements typically lead liquidity cycles, often initiating an upward trend before M2 reaches its peak, while consolidation is the main phase during liquidity peaks. Current indicators suggest that market liquidity is likely to expand further, indicating that Bitcoin still has upward potential. As Bitcoin reflects expectations of looser liquidity in advance, it may attract capital inflows. Furthermore, if stock market valuations are too high, some capital may shift to the Bitcoin market. The macroeconomic adjustment coefficient has been lowered to 25%, while the long-term outlook remains robust. The overall macroeconomic trend remains fundamentally unchanged compared to the previous quarter, with the general direction of interest rate cuts and liquidity expansion still clear. However, considering factors such as slowing institutional fund inflows, potential changes in the leadership of the Federal Reserve, and escalating geopolitical risks, we have lowered the macroeconomic adjustment coefficient from 35% to 25%. Despite the reduction, a positive adjustment of 25% still sends a positive signal. We believe that the advancement of regulatory policies and the continued expansion of M2 money supply will support the medium- to long-term upward trend of Bitcoin, and this core view remains unchanged. Support level: $84,000; Resistance level: $98,000. Beyond the macroeconomic factors, on-chain indicators are also releasing corresponding signals. During the market correction in November 2025, bargain hunting concentrated around the $84,000 price level, forming a clear support zone. Bitcoin's price has since rebounded above this zone. The $98,000 price level corresponds to the average holding cost of short-term holders, constituting a short-term psychological resistance level. On-chain indicators show that market sentiment is transitioning from short-term panic to a neutral range. Key market indicators such as the MVRV-Z metric (1.25), Unrealized Net Profit Margin (NUPL, 0.39), and Realized Profit/Loss Margin (aSOPR, 1.00) have all moved out of the undervalued range and into the equilibrium valuation range. While the probability of a significant rebound driven by panic selling is low given the current market sentiment, the current market structure still offers profit potential. Combined with the macroeconomic environment and regulatory developments mentioned earlier, the data model shows that the logic for Bitcoin's medium- to long-term price increase remains valid. The current market structure has changed significantly compared to previous cycles. The increased proportion of institutional and long-term capital has reduced the likelihood of panic selling previously dominated by retail investors. Recent market pullbacks have been largely completed through a gradual asset rebalancing process. Although short-term market volatility will persist, on-chain indicators and fund flow trends suggest that the overall long-term upward trend of Bitcoin remains intact.

Target price lowered to $185,500, bullish outlook unchanged

Based on our TVM valuation framework, our calculations for the market in Q1 2026 yield a neutral benchmark valuation of $145,000 for Bitcoin, down from $154,000 in our previous report. We revise our target price to $185,500 with a 0% fundamental adjustment and a 25% macro adjustment.

We have increased the fundamental adjustment from -2% to 0%. While Bitcoin network activity remained largely flat compared to the previous quarter, the renewed market focus on the BTCFi ecosystem partially offset the extreme signals from both bulls and bears.

Conversely, we have decreased the macro adjustment from 35% to 25%.

While factors such as expectations of interest rate cuts, M2 expansion, and the potential passage of the Clarity Act continue to support the market, slowing institutional inflows and rising geopolitical uncertainty necessitate a more conservative valuation approach. This target price reduction should not be interpreted as a bearish signal. Even after the adjustment, the valuation model still indicates approximately 100% upside potential for Bitcoin from its current price. The benchmark price reduction primarily reflects the impact of short-term market volatility, while Bitcoin's intrinsic value remains on an upward trend in the medium to long term. The recent price correction is a healthy market rebalancing process, and the medium- to long-term bullish trend for Bitcoin remains unchanged.

data.title: WhatsApp Rolls Back AI Bot Restrictions In Brazil After Regulator Steps In
data.description:
Brazil Pushes Back As WhatsApp Softens Its Stance

WhatsApp’s plans to curb third-party AI chatbots have hit fresh resistance, with Brazil emerging as a key flashpoint where regulators have forced Meta to pause enforcement and quietly roll back restrictions for local users.

Meta has confirmed that AI providers can continue operating chatbots for users with Brazilian phone numbers, days after the country’s competition authority stepped in to block new rules on WhatsApp’s Business API.

WhatsApp AI providers continue offering their chatbots to users with Brazilian phone numbers, days after the country’s competition regulator ordered the company to suspend its policy that bars 3rd-party, general-purpose chatbots from being offered on the app via its business API. pic.twitter.com/OCDzlm8D6A

— Bruno Dias (@brunodiasmz) January 15, 2026

The decision effectively freezes enforcement of a policy that would have limited access to general-purpose AI tools on the messaging platform.

According to a notice sent to developers and seen by TechCrunch, Meta told AI providers that they do not need to stop responding to users or display shutdown notices when messaging people with Brazil’s +55 country code.

The message states:

“The requirement to cease responding to user queries and implement pre-approved auto-reply language (mentioned below) before January 15, 2026, no longer applies when messaging people with a Brazil country code (+55).”

A Policy Meant To Restrict General AI Bots

Under Meta’s original plan, WhatsApp was set to block third-party, general-purpose chatbots from being offered through its Business API.

Tools such as ChatGPT and Grok were among those expected to be affected, while customer service bots used by businesses were always allowed to remain.

The policy officially takes effect on Thursday, 15 January, alongside a 90-day grace period.

@Microsoft@Copilot is leaving @WhatsApp on January 15, 2026, as @Meta enforces new platform policies that ban general-purpose AI chatbots from using WhatsApp Business API, affecting Copilot, ChatGPT, Perplexity, and others.

Nothing says "it's not personal, just business" quite… pic.twitter.com/HlHX4jHSkN

— Ikram Rana (@ikramrana) November 27, 2025

During that window, developers were instructed to stop replying to users and inform them that their chatbots would no longer function on WhatsApp.

Brazil is now exempt from those requirements, at least for the time being.

Competition Watchdog Raises Red Flags

Brazil’s competition authority, CADE, has opened a formal investigation into Meta’s updated WhatsApp terms.

The regulator is assessing whether the policy could restrict competition by limiting access for rival AI providers while favouring Meta AI, the company’s own chatbot built into WhatsApp.

CADE has also ordered a suspension of the new terms tied to WhatsApp Business in Brazil while the probe continues.

In its notice, the agency pointed to potential exclusionary practices that could harm fair access to users in a fast-growing AI market.

[ABA Antitrust Daily Digest January 13, 2026]

Brazil's antitrust body opens investigation into WhatsApp business tool

(Reuters) Brazil’s antitrust regulator CADE opened an investigation into Meta’s new WhatsApp Business terms and suspended their application in Brazil, citing… pic.twitter.com/xsEe4rDVYM

— ABA Antitrust Law Section (@abaantitrust) January 13, 2026

Italy And Europe Show A Familiar Pattern

Brazil is not alone.

Meta previously carved out a similar exemption in Italy after regulators raised concerns late last year.

The European Union has also intensified scrutiny of Meta’s AI strategy on WhatsApp, with antitrust authorities examining how Meta AI is being rolled out and promoted on the platform.

we won! 🌴

Italy just blocked Meta's plan to ban Meta AI competitors from WhatsApp.

Flew to Rome last week to present our case to the Italian Competition Authority.
Only three companies in the room:@Meta, @Interaction, and @OpenAI.

When I read Meta's defense of "AI is putting… https://t.co/PUTjHRm4dopic.twitter.com/vyjpRj74nq

— Marvin von Hagen (@marvinvonhagen) December 24, 2025

The repeated interventions suggest growing unease among regulators about large platforms using policy changes to reshape how AI services reach users, especially where the platform also offers its own competing product.

Meta Says AI Bots Strain WhatsApp’s Systems

Meta continues to defend its position, arguing that WhatsApp’s Business API was not designed to support high-volume, general-purpose AI chatbots.

The company says the surge in automated conversations has placed pressure on systems built mainly for customer support and business messaging.

Responding to CADE’s investigation, a WhatsApp spokesperson rejected claims of unfair conduct.

The spokesperson said,

“These claims are fundamentally flawed. The emergence of AI chatbots on our Business API put a strain on our systems that they were not designed to support.”

The spokesperson added that treating WhatsApp as a main distribution channel for AI tools misses the point.

“This logic assumes WhatsApp is somehow a de facto app store. The route to market for AI companies is the app stores themselves, their websites and industry partnerships; not the WhatsApp Business Platform.”

Why Brazil Matters For AI Providers

Brazil is one of WhatsApp’s largest markets globally, making the exemption particularly important for AI developers that rely on the app to reach users.

WhatsApp users:

1. 🇮🇳 India: 549.9 M
2. 🇧🇷 Brazil: 148 M
3. 🇮🇩 Indonesia: 112 M
4. 🇺🇸 USA: 100 M
5. 🇵🇭 Philippines: 88 M
6. 🇲🇽 Mexico: 77 M
7. 🇷🇺 Russia: 66.7 M
8. 🇹🇷 Turkey: 60 M
9. 🇪🇬 Egypt: 56 M
10. 🇵🇰 Pakistan: 52 M

Source: Statista, 2024-2025

— World of Statistics (@stats_feed) November 28, 2025

For now, general-purpose chatbots can continue operating without interruption for Brazilian users, preserving access for people who use these tools for information, work and experimentation.

Whether the pause turns into a longer-term rollback will depend on the outcome of CADE’s investigation.

What is clear is that Brazil’s intervention has forced Meta to slow down and rethink how its AI policies land in key markets, especially where competition concerns are front and centre.

data.title: NiceHash Debunks ‘Bitcoin Lottery’ as Untagged Blocks Spark Solo Miner Frenzy
data.description:
Solo Miner Strikes Bitcoin Lottery Rumour Unraveled

Social media briefly went into a frenzy this week after Bitcoin blocks 932129 and 932167 appeared without a clearly visible pool tag, fueling speculation that a solo miner had improbably struck it lucky — twice. The so-called “Bitcoin lottery” story quickly captured attention, even as the reality turned out to be far more mundane.

JUST IN: Solo Bitcoin miner mines a full block worth $295,000 (3.16 BTC)

Another Bitcoin lottery winner 🔥 pic.twitter.com/KGmVat1bPu

— Bitcoin Archive (@BitcoinArchive) January 13, 2026

In truth, both blocks had already been mined by NiceHash as part of internal testing for a forthcoming product. The temporary absence of a visible label on some blockchain explorers made the blocks appear unassigned, prompting viral speculation.

NiceHash clarified that the blocks were properly tagged on their end, and the display error stemmed from how certain explorers render metadata. The incident underscores how quickly gaps in onchain attribution can generate misleading narratives.

Because the blocks initially appeared “untagged,” many observers assumed they were mined independently, outside any known pool. This fed the idea that a solo miner had found two blocks consecutively — a statistical improbability that added drama to the story.

NiceHash operates a hashrate marketplace, connecting buyers of mining power with independent miners, rather than running a traditional pool. The company confirmed that the blocks were part of internal product tests, not the result of a lone miner hitting a jackpot.

NiceHash is the world’s leading hashrate marketplace, connecting sellers (miners) with buyers of hashrate.

Our platform offers a comprehensive toolkit for miners at every scale, streamlining operations and maximizing efficiency. Everything you need in one place:

✅Custom ASIC… pic.twitter.com/wZkz3D16ub

— NiceHash (@NiceHashMining) May 13, 2025

The episode highlights a key nuance in Bitcoin mining: pool or miner tags are voluntary metadata, not protocol-enforced identifiers. When a tag is missing or misread, attribution becomes a matter of interpretation rather than certainty, leaving room for speculation and sensationalism.

Chance vs. Strategy

The frenzy also reignited discussion around solo mining, where an individual miner works independently instead of contributing hashpower to a pool.

Solo mining is technically possible and occasionally successful — NiceHash noted that its services were involved in a substantial number of solo-found blocks in 2025 — but it is inherently highly unpredictable. Rewards can fluctuate dramatically, making solo mining more of a gamble than a sustainable business strategy.

Solo mining is rare, accounting for just 0.07% of blocks mined last year. Still, it's a growing movement as more people adopt home miners and learn about mining. #Bitcoin#SoloMiningpic.twitter.com/RXA0ObzTiD

— Digital Mining Solutions (@DigMinSolutions) January 13, 2026

Institutional miners, on the other hand, rely on large-scale operations and advanced strategies to reduce variance and generate predictable revenue streams. As Bitcoin block rewards shrink with each halving, these operators face growing margin pressure and tighter economics. Many are diversifying into adjacent fields like AI and high-performance computing to supplement mining profits.

INSTITUTIONAL INVESTORS HAVE BOUGHT ROUGHLY 6X THE NEWLY MINED BITCOIN SUPPLY IN 2026!

30K $BTC PURCHASED VS ONLY 5.7K MINED 🚀 pic.twitter.com/IVz0SNt2HQ

— That Martini Guy ₿ (@MartiniGuyYT) January 15, 2026

The NiceHash incident serves as a cautionary tale: even on an open blockchain, metadata gaps and misinterpretations can create viral “lottery” myths, and assumptions can spread faster than the underlying facts.

data.title: Is the Clarity Act a “crypto survival line”? Five key facts revealed.
data.description:
Author: Merkle3s Capital, Source: @Merkle3sCapital

Introduction: From "Stablecoins Cannot Pay Interest" to "Competition Methods Are Being Rewritten"

The discussion surrounding the U.S. *CLARITY Cryptocurrency Market Structure Act* is being dominated by a highly simplified, even emotional, narrative:

Stablecoins cannot pay interest, DeFi faces stricter regulations, and thus this act has quickly been labeled the "lifeline of crypto."

The problem with this argument is that it compresses an act that is essentially about **market structure, competition methods, and the boundaries of responsibility** into a binary judgment of "stricter or looser regulation."

The result not only amplified the panic but also obscured the structural contradictions that legislators were truly trying to address. Just as the controversy continued to escalate, Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong publicly announced the withdrawal of support for the current version of the Crypto Market Structure Act, stating bluntly, "I'd rather have no bill than a bad one." This statement doesn't mean the industry is rejecting regulation itself; rather, it indicates that the real disagreements only emerge when the bill moves from principled discussions to specific provisions, truly touching on business models and profit distribution. Returning to the original text of the CLARITY Act, it's clear that it doesn't attempt to stifle stablecoins or DeFi, but rather deliberately reshapes the competitive logic of the crypto industry. By prohibiting stablecoin incentives that reward "simply for holding," the act effectively blocks a path that could easily evolve into a subsidy race and vicious involution, forcing projects to shift their competitive focus from "who gives more" to "whose product, scenario, and network has greater long-term value." Similarly, in defining the responsibilities of DeFi, exchanges, and intermediaries, CLARITY focuses not on technological labels, but on whether control exists and responsibilities are clear. What is being challenged is not innovation itself, but the old growth model that has long relied on blurred boundaries and gray areas for expansion. This article will, based on the original draft of the CLARITY bill and the official Fact Sheet, break down what this bill truly aims to change, starting with five key facts often overlooked in mainstream interpretations, and why simply calling it the "crypto lifeline" might misjudge its true impact on the industry's future. Key Fact 1: The "de-yieldification" of stablecoins is to prevent vicious competition, not to stifle innovation. In all discussions about the CLARITY bill, "stablecoins cannot accrue interest" is undoubtedly one of the most contentious points. Many interpretations equate this directly to "the weakening of stablecoin appeal," "the decline in the competitiveness of crypto finance," and even elevate it to a "life-or-death line for the industry." However, a return to the text of the bill itself reveals this to be a clear misinterpretation. CLARITY does not prohibit all incentive behaviors related to stablecoins, but rather the single model of paying users interest or returns solely for holding payment-type stablecoins. In other words, what is being rejected is not competition itself, but a form of competition that easily devolves into vicious involution. In the exceptions listed in the bill, the legislators explicitly retain various reward spaces based on usage behavior, including incentives related to payments, transfers, transactions, and settlements, as well as membership, promotions, cashback, and loyalty programs; at the same time, it does not prohibit all behavioral rewards such as providing liquidity, collateral, staking, and participation in governance. The logic behind this is not complicated. Once "holding for immediate returns" is allowed, competition among stablecoins quickly degenerates into a race to subsidize interest rates: whoever can subsidize more interest attracts more scale; and scale advantage, in turn, strengthens capital concentration, ultimately squeezing the survival space of small and medium-sized issuers. This competition does not rely on product innovation and is unrelated to real-world use cases, yet it continuously amplifies systemic risks. CLARITY's choice is to cut off this easiest, but also most dangerous, growth path at the institutional level, forcing stablecoin projects and platforms back to a healthier dimension of competition: whoever can build real-world payment scenarios, whoever can create network effects, and whoever can retain users through product design and marketing capabilities. From this perspective, the "de-yieldification" of stablecoins is not a suppression of innovation, but rather a forced innovation. It avoids forcing all stablecoins into the same "easy money" competition, and also creates space for differentiated products and real-world usage needs. This is one of the real reasons why the current bill has caused controversy. For platforms that rely on stablecoin incentives and financial subsidies as core growth tools, this adjustment means that their business models need to be restructured; while for legislators who want stablecoins to return to their role as payment and settlement infrastructure, this is precisely the structural goal the bill is trying to achieve. Key Fact Two: The core of CLARITY is not "stricter or looser regulation," but whether its boundaries are predictable. Beyond stablecoins, another major misinterpretation of CLARITY is that it has been simply understood as "comprehensive tightening of regulation," and even seen as a systemic crackdown on the crypto industry. However, if we consider the overall structure of the bill, this judgment itself is untenable. The core issue that CLARITY seeks to address is not the intensity of regulation itself, but rather the regulatory uncertainty that has long plagued the crypto industry. For a considerable period, the legal attributes of digital assets have often been passively defined through enforcement actions rather than explicitly stipulated rules. Project teams and platforms could only operate in a gray area until one day they were told, "You were never compliant in the first place." The key change in CLARITY is that it transforms this uncertainty at the institutional level into a rule path that is **selectable beforehand and verifiable afterward**. This judgment is not a subjective interpretation, but is directly reflected in the structural design of the entire bill. CLARITY does not tighten or loosen regulation through a single clause, but instead replaces the past practice of heavily relying on enforcement discretion with categorized, specialized, and path-based rules. Specifically: At the asset attribute level, the bill attempts to end long-standing regulatory ambiguity and institutional strife by clearly distinguishing between digital asset securities and digital asset commodities and requiring regulatory agencies to develop enforceable judgment standards; At the regulatory division level, the bill clarifies the regulatory framework corresponding to different types of digital asset activities, elevating the question of "who regulates what" from practical disputes to institutional arrangements; At the compliance path level, the bill introduces predictable institutional choices, requiring projects to clearly define their own positioning under disclosure obligations, liability attribution, and anti-circumvention rules, rather than passively waiting for ex-post determination; Regarding its temporal effect, the bill explicitly states that it has no retroactive effect, avoiding the redefinition of past behavior through legislation and emphasizing the forward application of rules. These provisions do not point to a single direction of "stricter" or "looserer," but rather collectively serve a core objective: to replace enforcement discretion with rules, and to replace uncertain risks with path selection. Regulation will no longer appear in the form of sudden enforcement, but will be pre-written into the system, becoming a prerequisite for market participation. This also means that CLARITY is not lowering industry barriers. On the contrary, it requires more systematic information disclosure, clearer boundaries of responsibility, and explicit restrictions on evasive behavior. The difference is that these costs are no longer unpredictable risks, but institutional constraints that can be assessed, chosen, and priced. From this perspective, the real impact of CLARITY is not "relaxing regulation" or "tightening regulation," but rather a clear institutional turning point: the crypto industry will shift from relying on vague boundaries and regulatory arbitrage to competing under clear rules. For projects that rely on narrative uncertainty, compliance gray areas, or regulatory delays to survive, this is undoubtedly a squeeze; but for participants who hope to operate long-term, attract institutional funding, and expand their business within a clear legal framework, this shift itself is the very meaning of the market structure bill. Key Fact Three: DeFi was not denied, but "control" was written into the bill's text for the first time. In discussions about CLARITY, DeFi was often placed in a dichotomous narrative of "either being allowed or being stifled." However, judging from the original text of the bill, this dichotomy is also inaccurate. CLARITY did not adopt a "one-size-fits-all" approach to DeFi, but instead, for the first time, clearly distinguished between two types of protocols at the legislative level: **decentralized financial transaction protocols** and **non-decentralized financial transaction protocols**. The real dividing line is not whether smart contracts are used, nor whether it claims to be "decentralized," but whether **control actually exists**. According to the bill's definition, a financial protocol considered "decentralized" should operate based on pre-set, automatically executed, non-discretionary rules, and should not rely on any individual or organization to custody or control user assets. In other words, the execution result of the protocol should not depend on the judgment or approval of any single entity. Conversely, if an individual or specific group can substantially control or alter the functionality of a protocol, restricting, censoring, or preventing user participation, the protocol may be deemed a "non-decentralized financial transaction protocol," thus incurring compliance obligations similar to those of intermediaries. This definition has a structural impact on the DeFi ecosystem. Many designs considered reasonable or even necessary in engineering practice, such as multi-signature governance, upgradeable contracts, emergency suspension mechanisms, or front-end interfaces maintained by the core team, may be seen as manifestations of "control" from a legal perspective. Technical security considerations do not necessarily equate to legal decentralization. CLARITY does not completely ignore real-world risks. In the legislation, legislators have provided limited buffer space for protocols, allowing for cybersecurity or emergency response measures under predefined, rule-based, and temporary conditions, without automatically sacrificing their decentralized attributes. However, this exemption itself emphasizes a premise: **control must be strictly constrained within the rules and cannot evolve into routine intervention.** From the outcome, CLARITY did not deny the legitimacy of DeFi, but rather forced the entire industry to directly answer a long-avoided question: **When a protocol malfunctions, who has the right to press the "button"?** If this power truly exists, then the corresponding responsibility cannot be indefinitely outsourced to the "code." Therefore, the true meaning of this chapter is not "whether DeFi will be regulated," but rather: **which DeFi designs can continue to be considered infrastructure without intermediary responsibility, and which must reassess their legal status.** This also means that **future competition in DeFi will not only be a competition of products and efficiency, but also a competition of governance structures and the ability to minimize control.** Key Fact Four: The Real Focus of Targeted Regulation Is Not on Crypto Innovation, but on "Gray Entry Points" and Enforcement Blind Spots. Contrary to the initial impression that the crypto industry is facing comprehensive tightening, CLARITY's regulatory focus is actually very concentrated. From the text of the bill and the official Fact Sheet, it's clear that the focus and repeated emphasis are not on on-chain innovation itself, but rather on gray entry points and enforcement blind spots that have long existed in a regulatory gray area. These provisions mainly focus on three levels. First, digital asset intermediaries and fiat currency entry points. CLARITY explicitly incorporates a range of intermediary activities related to digital assets into its financial regulatory framework, requiring them to fulfill obligations such as anti-money laundering, sanctions compliance, and record keeping. This is not an introduction of entirely new regulatory logic, but rather a systematic extension of existing rules in traditional finance to the point where crypto assets intersect with fiat currency. Secondly, there is the definition of responsibility at the front-end and application layers. Under the CLARITY framework, front-end interfaces and application services operated by US individuals or institutions to connect users with on-chain protocols are no longer inherently exempt from liability as "purely technical tools." When these front-ends possess substantial control, screening, or intervention capabilities, their compliance responsibilities will no longer be entirely outsourced to the underlying protocol. Thirdly, there is specific regulation for cash-related and high-risk channels. The bill sets forth clearer regulatory requirements and research obligations for digital asset ATMs, kiosks, and other cash entry points, as well as high-risk infrastructure such as coin mixers and anonymity enhancement tools. The aim is to reduce the operational space for money laundering, fraud, and sanctions evasion. It's important to note that these measures are not aimed at "decentralized technology" itself, but rather at participants who act as entry points, intermediaries, or controllers in the real world. CLARITY's focus remains on: when crypto assets connect with the real financial system, whoever acts as an intermediary must bear corresponding responsibility. From an impact perspective, the direct impact of these provisions on mainstream crypto innovation is relatively limited, but they will significantly increase the operating costs of gray channels. For business models that rely on regulatory gaps, cross-border ambiguity, or enforcement difficulties, this represents a structural squeeze; while for projects and platforms hoping to operate long-term in a compliant environment, it's more like an "environmental cleanup." Therefore, the message CLARITY conveys in this chapter is not "restricting innovation," but rather drawing a clearer bottom line: innovation can continue, but gray areas can no longer linger indefinitely outside of regulation. Key Fact Five: The real disagreement lies not in "whether or not to regulate," but in who bears the costs. Based on the first four key facts, the regulatory logic outlined by CLARITY has gradually become clear: stablecoins are forced to return to their role as payment tools, the regulatory boundary is shifting from enforcement discretion to rule selection, the legitimacy of DeFi depends on the control structure, and gray areas are being systematically compressed. Against this backdrop, Coinbase's withdrawal of support for the bill is no longer an isolated incident, but a concentrated manifestation of the entire controversy. Judging from public statements, the focus of opposition is not on "whether regulatory clarity is needed," but rather on the impact of specific provisions on existing business models, including stablecoin incentive arrangements, the definition of DeFi boundaries, the feasibility of tokenized assets, and the redistribution of regulatory power among different institutions. The essence of these disagreements is not a clash of value positions, but rather how costs and responsibilities are redistributed within the industry. CLARITY does not deny the existence of the crypto industry, but it is doing something more impactful: bringing the long-term externalized compliance costs, systemic risks, and intermediary responsibilities back onto specific business entities. This means that models that relied on subsidies, regulatory ambiguity, or structural gray areas for growth will face significant pressure; while those participants who can operate under clear rules and incorporate compliance costs into their long-term business models are more likely to gain sustainable growth. Therefore, the real disagreement surrounding CLARITY is not about whether regulation will stifle innovation, but rather: who will pay the price for the industry's move towards institutionalized competition, and who will reap the benefits of the new order. Conclusion: CLARITY is not a "life-or-death line" for crypto, but a watershed moment for the old growth model. Returning to the initial question: Is CLARITY a "life-or-death line" for the crypto industry? Looking at the text and overall structure of the bill, the answer is not between "life" and "death," but rather a clear shift. CLARITY does not attempt to end the crypto industry, but rather delineates boundaries and rewrites rules at the institutional level, forcing market participants to face a reality: uncertainty is being gradually replaced by responsibility. What this bill truly ends is not innovation itself, but the old growth model that relies on subsidies, blurred boundaries, and outsourcing of responsibility. What is preserved is the space for competition under clear rules, a necessary stage for the crypto industry to mature. Therefore, simply understanding CLARITY as "tightening regulation" or an "industry life-or-death line" underestimates its true impact. It's more like a watershed: on one side is the past that relied on gray areas and narrative advantages; on the other side is a future that needs to redefine competitiveness under institutional constraints.

data.title: From Hope to Frustration: The Struggle Behind the Delay of the Clarity Bill
data.description:
Written by: 0xjs@Jinse Finance

The Crypto Market Structure Act is stalled in the Senate.

The Senate version of the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act (CLARITY Act) suffered a major setback just before its markup, leading to an emergency postponement of the Senate Banking Committee markup originally scheduled for January 15 (currently expected to be postponed to the end of January or later).

This article will first examine the current progress of the CLARITY Act, and then focus on its main points of contention and the viewpoints of both supporters and opponents.

Current Progress of the CLARITY Act

The House of Representatives has passed: The bill passed the House of Representatives in July 2025 with a vote of 294-134, making it another important piece of crypto market structure legislation after the GENIUS Act (which was signed into law by President Trump in July 2025).

Senate Stage: The bill is currently under review by the Senate Banking Committee. Originally scheduled for a markup (review, amendment, and vote) on January 15, 2026, it has been postponed to late January or later due to strong industry opposition and a failure to reach an agreement between the two parties.

Key Event Triggering the Delay: Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong publicly stated on X on January 14 that he could not support the current draft, listing several major issues, after which the committee quickly announced a delay. This event garnered tens of thousands of likes, sparking widespread industry discussion and media coverage.

Short-Term Outlook: Both the Senate Banking Committee and the Agriculture Committee (responsible for the CFTC section) need to advance the bill. Experts estimate a 50-60% probability of passage in 2026, but midterm election political factors and a packed schedule could further delay it to 2027. White House crypto advisor David Sacks and others are still actively pushing for its passage.

Key Controversies of the CLARITY Bill

The CLARITY Bill is currently stuck in the most intense stage of the debate, with the main points of contention highly concentrated. The following are some of the most crucial and explosive aspects:

1. Stablecoin Yield/Rewards – The Core “Banks vs. Crypto” War

  • Essence of the Controversy: The draft bill significantly restricts or substantially prohibits passive yields on stablecoins, allowing only rewards based on “activities” (such as trading, governance, and staking).

  • The Position of Banks/Traditional Financial Lobbying Groups (53+ organizations including the American Bankers Association exerting joint pressure): If stablecoins can pay interest, they will absorb bank deposits like “shadow banks” (estimated to be a loss of $1-1.5 trillion in deposits), threatening financial stability and the survival of community banks.

    Crypto industry perspective (Coinbase, DeFi projects, most native communities): This is tantamount to banks eliminating competitors and stifling DeFi's core competitiveness; the US is damaging the global status of the dollar; Coinbase calls this "the biggest regulatory capture by bank protectionism ever." This is the main trigger for Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong's public reversal and the delay of the markup. 2. Substantive Restrictions on Tokenized Equities / RWA (Tokenized Equities/Real-World Assets) The essence of the controversy: The draft is accused of containing a de facto ban or extremely high barriers, severely restricting the tokenization of traditional stocks, equities, bonds, and other assets on the blockchain. Industry opposition: This directly hits the biggest growth point in the RWA sector in the future, hindering blockchain from bringing capital markets onto the chain; Coinbase claims this will cause the US to fall behind in the race for next-generation financial infrastructure. Supporters/Regulators: Concerned about circumventing securities laws, increasing systemic risk and investor protection loopholes. 3. DeFi Regulatory Intensity and Privacy Erosion The essence of the controversy: Imposing strict AML/KYC and reporting obligations on decentralized protocols, potentially even requiring protocol parties to provide "unlimited access to users' financial records." Critics (DeFi fundamentalists, Coinbase, etc.): This destroys the core values ​​of DeFi (privacy, self-custody, permissionless), forcing decentralized protocols into "regulated entities," and many protocols may directly withdraw from the US. The other side (Some Democratic lawmakers, former SEC officials): The current draft provides insufficient exemptions for DeFi/software developers and weak investor protection, potentially leading to a "next FTX"-style fraud. 4. Imbalance in Power Distribution Between SEC and CFTC The essence of the controversy: The draft is accused of significantly weakening the CFTC's authority over "digital goods," instead allowing the SEC to dominate more areas (especially the early "ancillary asset" stage). Mainstream view in the crypto industry: The CFTC is more friendly and better suited for innovation; the SEC's approach is tantamount to continuing to regulate crypto as securities, effectively stifling it. Regulatory conservatives: Concerned that CFTC regulation is too lenient and investor protection is insufficient. 5. Other secondary but still sharp disagreements Former SEC chief accountant and others warn that the draft is "seriously inadequate," with weak disclosure, auditing, and enforcement tools that could lead to systemic fraud. Ethics/Conflict of Interest Clause: Democrats wanted to include a clause prohibiting crypto interests of the Trump family and officials, which Republicans strongly opposed, viewing it as a "partisan attack." State Law Preemption: Whether federal uniform rules fully cover state laws sparked discontent among state regulators. The CLARITY Act, originally the biggest hope for ending regulatory uncertainty, has now become a polarized conflict at the last minute due to the fundamental conflict between banking interests and native crypto innovation: "better no bill than a bad one." Coinbase's tough stance led to a direct delay for markup, making it difficult for the bill to pass quickly in the short term, but negotiations continue—the final version will likely require significant concessions on stablecoin yield and DeFi freedom to get back on track. **Summary of Core Viewpoints of Supporters and Opponents** **Supporters (Those still actively pushing for or willing to continue negotiating amendments)** These groups mostly believe that "clear rules are better than a regulatory vacuum/enforcement-style regulation," and are willing to accept certain compromises in exchange for overall legitimacy and institutional funding. **Ripple (Brad Garlinghouse):** This is "a significant step forward," clear rules are better than chaos; Coinbase's fierce opposition surprised them, but the rest of the industry is still "constructively participating"; the problem can be solved through the markup process. **Circle (USDC issuer):** The bill provides an overall workable framework, which will help the legitimate large-scale adoption of stablecoins; willing to continue negotiations. **Kraken:** Supports advancing the bill, believing that a clear division of labor between the SEC and CFTC is beneficial to the industry in the long run. Top venture capital firms like a16z (Andreessen Horowitz) and Paradigm: They hope to see institutional compliance pathways opened up, facilitating the entry of large funds; they believe that while the current draft has flaws, it is heading in the right direction. Industry associations/think tanks like Coin Center and Digital Chamber: The bill can end the nightmare of "enforcement-style regulation," providing predictability, which is essential for the US to maintain its global competitiveness in the crypto space. Some Republican lawmakers (such as Senate Banking Committee Chairman Tim Scott and Cynthia Lummis): The bill replaces uncertainty, provides clear rules, strengthens anti-fraud measures, and brings businesses back to the US. In short, the supporters' stance is: "We'd rather have clear rules with flaws than a perpetual gray area plus enforcement-style regulation; we're willing to accept some concessions in exchange for legitimacy and institutional funding." The opponents (strongly opposed to the current version, even preferring no legislation at all) prioritize protecting the core values ​​of cryptography (privacy, self-custody, decentralization, freedom of innovation, and equal competition), believing the current draft is "worse than the status quo." Coinbase (Brian Armstrong) is the loudest and most influential voice of opposition, with core reasons for opposition (directly quoted from their X post): **Essentially banning tokenized equities (a major blow to tokenized stocks/RWA)** **Imposing prohibitive clauses on DeFi is tantamount to giving the government "unlimited access to users' financial records," destroying privacy rights** **Weakening the CFTC's authority, making it subordinate to the SEC, stifling innovation** **Banning/strictly limiting passive yields (yield/rewards) of stablecoins allows banks to eliminate competitors, damaging the global status of the US dollar** **Attitude:** Preferring no legislation to bad legislation; however, they remain open to negotiations, hoping for a better version. **A large number of DeFi fundamentalists, Bitcoin maximalists, and self-custodian advocates:** The bill is a "wolf in sheep's clothing," superficially providing clear rules but actually tailored for banking interests, sacrificing decentralization and user privacy in exchange for compliance. **Some privacy coin/heavy decentralized project communities:** They worry that stricter retroactive requirements will lead to delisting or an existential crisis. **A one-sentence summary of the opposition's position:** "The current version is more dangerous than a regulatory vacuum; it will turn crypto into 'regulated securities' controlled by banks, sacrificing core freedom and innovation; without good rules, we'll maintain the status quo and continue the fight." **Conclusion:** The CLARITY Act is no longer a united front of "crypto vs. regulation." Instead, it has evolved into a debate within the crypto community between "decentralization idealists" and "institutional compliance pragmatists." Bank lobbying groups, on the other hand, play a key role in disrupting this balance behind the scenes.

data.title: US Lawmakers in West Virginia Consider Using Public Funds for Bitcoin Gold and Regulated Stablecoins
data.description:
West Virginia Considers Investing State Funds in Bitcoin and Gold

A US state in the Appalachian region, West Virginia may soon allow its treasury to invest in Bitcoin and precious metals as part of a bid to diversify public funds and hedge against inflation.

Senate Bill 143, known as the Inflation Protection Act of 2026, proposes permitting the state treasury to allocate up to 10% of its portfolio into selected digital assets and gold.

Bitcoin Could Become Part of Public Reserves

Under the bill, digital assets eligible for investment must have a market capitalization exceeding $750 billion from the previous calendar year.

NEW: West Virginia Introduces Bitcoin & Gold Treasury Bill

West Virginia lawmakers have introduced Senate Bill 143, the Inflation Protection Act of 2026, which would allow the state Treasury to allocate a portion of state funds to Bitcoin and gold as an inflation hedge.

The… pic.twitter.com/rjb5Cr6Ra2

— Bitcoin News (@BitcoinNewsCom) January 15, 2026

Currently, Bitcoin is the only cryptocurrency meeting this threshold, positioning it as the primary digital asset under consideration.

Any Bitcoin acquired would need to be held by a qualified custodian, in an exchange-traded product, or via secure custody solutions.

Stablecoins would also be eligible if they have regulatory approval from federal or state authorities.

Gold Paired With Crypto to Manage Volatility

Alongside Bitcoin, the proposal includes gold as a traditional hedge against inflation.

Lawmakers suggest that pairing a highly volatile asset like Bitcoin with gold could balance potential risk with stability.

West Virginia Senate introduces 'Inflation Protection Act' Bill to allow public funds to be invested in precious metals and $BTC.

The states are coming. pic.twitter.com/uKo1Vkcumj

— LuxAlgo (@LuxAlgo) January 15, 2026

Supporters of the bill have noted,

“The legislation reflects a growing recognition of Bitcoin as digital gold.”

The move was both a financial and symbolic gesture toward emerging asset classes.

Legislative Path and State-Level Precedents

Senator Chris Rose introduced the bill to the West Virginia legislature on Wednesday, and it has been referred to the Committee on Banking and Insurance for review.

While the proposal is still in committee, the measure follows a trend in other US states.

Texas, Arizona, and New Hampshire have already passed legislation permitting state treasuries to invest in cryptocurrencies, while several other states have considered similar bills in 2025.

Earlier this year, New Hampshire and Arizona became the 1st and 2nd states to pass ‘Strategic Bitcoin Reserve’ legislation into law.

Texas is poised to be the third. But there is a 4th and 5th state that could pull the trigger in 2025.

Do not sleep on the states. 🇺🇸 pic.twitter.com/21oreHKW19

— Dennis Porter (@Dennis_Porter_) June 17, 2025

Balancing Innovation With Risk

Critics caution that Bitcoin’s notorious price swings could pose a risk to taxpayer funds, citing periods of double-digit monthly changes.

The bill addresses this by including the $750 billion market cap requirement and pairing Bitcoin with gold, aiming to filter out less mature assets and provide a counterweight to volatility.

National Context as Crypto Regulation Evolves

The introduction of the Inflation Protection Act comes as federal discussions on digital asset regulation continue.

The US Senate recently postponed a markup of the CLARITY Act, legislation intended to establish clearer digital asset market structures.

Lawmakers and industry observers have raised concerns over stablecoin rewards, decentralized finance provisions, and regulatory oversight.

US: Senate delays work on the “Clarity Act” after criticism from Coinbase (Armstrong).

Background tensions include stablecoin rewards, tokenized equities, and the SEC vs. CFTC jurisdiction split. pic.twitter.com/Rsdkzo1Rn8

— 2TheMoon (@2TheMoonLexA) January 15, 2026

West Virginia’s bill adds another layer to the ongoing debate about the role of digital assets in public finance.

Potential for Broader Influence

If passed, the legislation could make West Virginia one of the first states to formally include Bitcoin in its treasury strategy, potentially influencing other states to consider digital assets alongside traditional reserves.

The bill reflects a cautious approach, aiming to combine innovation with safeguards to protect public funds while signalling openness to emerging financial technologies.

data.title: From "I'm coming!" to "Binance Life": An Experiment in the Financialization of Attention
data.description:
Author:Clow

On January 1, 2026, Binance co-founder He Yi posted a new year tweet:“I'm here, damn it!”

Rough and direct, it's just like the collective venting of crypto traders after experiencing a bear market.

Subsequently, the meme of the same name on BSC began to surge, officially launching on Binance Alpha on January 6th, with a maximum increase of over 800% within 24 hours. This is not an isolated case. Within just two weeks, a batch of meme tokens with strong Chinese internet DNA, such as "Life K-line" and "Dark Horse," successively exploded, along with previous ones like "Binance Life" and "Hakimi," causing BSC on-chain gas consumption and transaction volume to rise exponentially. Some exclaimed that overnight, "the whole world is speaking Chinese." How exactly did this collective frenzy of Chinese memes occur? The Liquidity Wars: BSC's Counterattack The story begins with Solana. Starting in the second half of 2024, Solana, with its low gas fees and extremely fast transaction speeds, virtually monopolized the meme market. The Pump.fun model flourished, with tens of thousands of new coins being created, listed, and then wiped out daily. However, when everyone was fighting on the same track, the "PVP" game became extremely involutionary, and the profit-loss ratio for retail investors continued to worsen. The market began to crave a new narrative and a new battlefield. Binance astutely recognized this signal. As the world's largest centralized exchange, it faces the challenge of DEXs eroding pricing power over long-tail assets. The launch of the Binance Alpha platform is essentially a "co-opting" of on-chain native liquidity—lowering the user entry barrier through Web3 wallets and locking liquidity back within the BSC ecosystem. Thus, a massive migration of funds quietly occurred. Many traders began turning their attention to BSC. The reason is simple: Making money on Solana by relying on speed and information asymmetry is becoming increasingly difficult, while BSC, backed by Binance, offers a clearer "upward trajectory." This is a typical example of capital rotation under a "zero-sum game." When the profit-loss ratio of an ecosystem continues to deteriorate, smart money will always be the first to seek the next battlefield. Binance Alpha provides a clear "airdrop expectation" as a guaranteed return—even if the token itself performs poorly, participants can still obtain other rewards from the platform through the points system. This "safety net" mechanism greatly lowers the psychological barrier to capital migration. More notably, a significant characteristic of this round of market activity is its "Chinese dominance." Unlike the previous Doge or Pepe narratives dominated by Western communities, the pricing power of Meme coins on BSC has, for the first time, clearly tilted towards the Eastern community. This is not only due to the Chinese background of the Binance founding team, but also because, against the backdrop of liquidity depletion, the Chinese community has demonstrated stronger organizational and mobilization capabilities and a stronger sense of cultural identity. The Meme-Making Ability of CZ and He Yi In the Meme coin market, attention equals liquidity. Within the BSC ecosystem, the social media activities of Zhao Changpeng (CZ) and He Yi have effectively become market "barometers." Every emoji and every reply they make is instantly interpreted by the community as an asset pricing signal. He Yi is the core catalyst for this round of market activity. The creation of "Binance Life" is a prime example. The story began with He Yi's joking remark on social media, "Wishing you a Binance Life." The community quickly interpreted it as "Binance Life vs. Other Life," and a token of the same name was born. Subsequently, OKX founder Star and Base founder Jess shared the post, escalating the narrative into an "industry-wide meme." This is not just a joke, but a form of identity. Holding "Binance Life" represents loyalty to the BNB ecosystem and a desire to achieve upward mobility through Binance. In the crypto world, "choosing which exchange" is no longer merely a tool selection, but a declaration of identity. It's worth noting that the popularity of "Binance Life" also reflects a unique dissemination mechanism: Key figure interaction → Community token creation → Peer imitation → Narrative upgrade. This chain transformed a simple joke into an industry-wide topic within just a few days, causing its market value to soar. Although CZ has stepped down as CEO, his status as a spiritual leader remains unshakeable. On January 14, 2026, his Chinese AMA held at Binance Square set the tone for the BSC Meme ecosystem. He explicitly stated: "Truly successful Meme coins need historical narratives and cultural references." This assertion is highly instructive. It directly negates those "country bumpkin" projects that lack cultural core and rely purely on price manipulation, while affirming those Memes that can carry broad cultural consensus and evoke collective emotional resonance—whether it's a comeback narrative, identity recognition, or a subcultural symbol. Listing Expectations: The Potential of BSC Meme However, founder endorsements alone are insufficient to sustain a sector's continued rise. The core reason for BSC Meme's potential lies in its clear listing path: Binance Alpha → Binance Futures → Binance Spot. What does this path mean? For on-chain native tokens, being listed on Binance Alpha is already the first step towards official recognition; excellent performance offers the opportunity to be listed on the Binance Futures market, gaining greater liquidity and trading depth; and the ultimate goal is to be listed on Binance Spot—which is practically equivalent to obtaining a "graduation certificate" from a major exchange. This clear upward channel provides BSC Meme with sustained price expectations. From a market microstructure perspective, this listing path creates a "step-by-step unlocking" game mechanism. Each listing announcement brings a surge of new buying as investors anticipate a larger liquidity injection in the next phase. On-chain data shows that many tokens experience dramatic turnover after news of their Binance Alpha listings, indicating that significant funds were preemptively positioned for the announcements. The brilliance of this mechanism lies in its ability to break down what would otherwise be a one-off "listing boost" into multiple stages, each generating a new wave of FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) sentiment. For market makers and early holders, this means more opportunities to exit; for retail investors, it means "there's always another positive development to look forward to." The investor's logic is simple: as long as there's an expectation of listing, someone will be willing to buy. In contrast, Meme coins on Solana lack such a clear "exit path," relying more on spontaneous community dissemination and short-term speculative hype, often resulting in extremely short lifecycles. The Cultural Code of Chinese Memes Of course, not all BSC Memes succeed. Looking at this round of market activity, truly vibrant tokens often have a cultural core as their foundation. Take "Binance Life" as an example; its success stems from its precise targeting of the collective psychology within the Chinese-speaking crypto community—the desire for financial freedom, the imagination of upward social mobility, and the identification with being an "insider." From a broader perspective, the cultural code of this round of Chinese memes can be summarized in several dimensions: Identity-based: Linking holding a particular token to a specific identity, forming a "us vs. them" group division. Holding means taking sides; taking sides means belonging.

  • Emotional catharsis type:Carries the collective emotions of crypto traders after experiencing market volatility, whether it's the anxiety after missing out, the elation after making a fortune, or the self-deprecation after being trapped.

  • Subculture breaking into the mainstream type:Leveraging external traffic such as anime, cute pets, and internet memes, Meme coin has the opportunity to reach user groups outside the crypto circle, gaining a wider base of dissemination. These tokens share a common thread: they are not merely strings of code or symbols, but rather embody a certain cultural consensus or emotional projection. As CZ stated, truly successful memes need "historical narratives and cultural references." However, retail investors must remain vigilant in this feast. Firstly, there is the risk of a "single point of failure." The valuation logic of the entire Chinese meme sector is highly dependent on the personal influence of He Yi and CZ. If they reduce their interaction, or if regulatory pressure forces them to remain silent, the value support for these tokens will collapse instantly. Secondly, there's the "sell the news" effect. Data shows that many tokens often reach short-term highs after listing on Binance Alpha. Early profit-takers use this opportunity to distribute their holdings to retail investors who bought in anticipation of the listing. It's a brutal but real game: by the time you see the "listing" news, smart money may already be selling. Thirdly, there's the vulnerability of liquidity. Although BSC Meme attracted a large amount of capital in the short term, most of this capital was speculative and lacked long-term holding intentions. Once market sentiment shifts, or a more attractive new narrative emerges, funds may quickly withdraw, causing a precipitous price drop. Finally, there's the shadow of regulation. CZ repeatedly mentioned compliance and risk in his AMA, implying that Binance must carefully avoid the red lines of global regulatory agencies while promoting the Meme craze. If Chinese Meme coins are classified as unregistered securities issued through centralized platforms, the entire sector will face severe challenges. Returning to the initial question: What exactly is this Chinese Meme frenzy? It's an experiment in the "financialization of attention," jointly constructed by exchange infrastructure, elite narratives, and grassroots cultural resonance. In this cyber casino built on algorithms, points, and Twitter, the real winners are always those who build the stage and those who hear the music first. Most latecomers chasing trends may ultimately only get an expensive "life chart" experience. After all, in the crypto world, the price of a meme is highly uncertain, but the platform's cut is certain.

data.title: Inside Job or Luck? Polymarket Whales Profit Millions Ahead of Maduro Arrest
data.description:
Insider Makes $400,000 on Maduro's Capture At Polymarket

Three major traders on Polymarket has reportedly hit the jackpot after placing a high-stakes bet on the arrest of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro just hours before his arrest, raising suspicions of insider trading.

Betting on Venezuela’s Political Crisis — Prediction, or Just Perfect Timing?
In December 2025, a new account appeared on Polymarket. Instead of spreading bets around, it focused on one thing only: Venezuela’s political and military future.

The questions were straightforward.… pic.twitter.com/HiPtoJf2kT

— CoinHub Korea (@CoinhubK) January 8, 2026

On-chain analysts flagged three wallets that generated abnormal returns during the Venezuela crisis. One trader reportedly withdrew nearly $195,000 before disappearing from the network, while two other wallets have remained inactive for over a week.

A third trader, who goes by the pseudonym Sbet365, continues to place sizable bets today, including one on the potential removal of Iran’s supreme leader Ali Khamenei by the end of January, a market that has already seen $28 million in trading volume.

Insider who made $400,000 on Maduro's capture at Polymarket – could turn out to be someone connected to Steven Charles Witkoff (WLFI Co-Founder)

The entire CT was talking yesterday about an insider who placed around $32k just a few hours before the well–known events in… pic.twitter.com/IXfTYOTMZs

— Andrew 10 GWEI (@Andrey_10gwei) January 4, 2026

The timing and precision of these positions have raised suspicions that privileged, non-public information may have been exploited for financial gain. Former President Donald Trump added fuel to the debate by claiming that Venezuelan “leakers” were imprisoned, hinting that insider knowledge could have filtered into political and crypto circles prior to official announcements.

The incident underscores a growing tension within decentralized prediction markets. Platforms like Polymarket rely on blockchain’s public ledger to ensure transparency, yet proving that traders acted on insider information remains difficult without off-chain evidence.

The event highlights the fine line between legitimate predictive betting and manipulative trading, particularly as crypto whales increasingly move beyond cryptocurrency price speculation into political and geopolitical wagers.

New Polymarket: Who Will Strike Iran First?

what happened:
iran is currently overwhelmed by protests that began on december 28, 2025. the iranian regime has responded with extreme brutality, with the death toll already in the thousands. yesterday, on january 13, trump declared… pic.twitter.com/C7LBkqm07j

— winngamer (@winngamerx) January 14, 2026

Regulators and industry observers are now questioning whether decentralized platforms can effectively self-regulate. Can transparency alone deter participants with privileged insights, or is centralized oversight necessary? As prediction markets expand into politically sensitive areas, these questions are likely to shape the next wave of regulation in the crypto ecosystem.

The Maduro betting controversy serves as a reminder that even in transparent, blockchain-based markets, the combination of anonymity, high stakes, and geopolitical events can create fertile ground for speculation, manipulation, and regulatory scrutiny.

data.title: X ban sparks controversy over InfoFi Kaito's early shipments.
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

On January 15, 2026, Nikita Bier, product manager at X, announced that the social media platform will ban applications that reward users for posting on the site. Bier specifically mentioned that X will block the "InfoFi" application and its developers from accessing its API.

Affected by this news, as of press time, according to CoinGecko data, the market capitalization of the InfoFi sector has fallen by nearly 11%.

As a result of this news, as of press time, the market capitalization of the InfoFi sector has fallen by nearly 11%.

What is InfoFi? What other InfoFi projects are there? What do industry insiders think of the X ban?

I. What is InfoFi?

InfoFi (Information Finance) is a field within the Web3 ecosystem that utilizes financial mechanisms to extract, aggregate, and disseminate valuable information as a public good.

I. What is InfoFi?

InfoFi (Information Finance) is a field within the Web3 ecosystem that uses financial mechanisms to extract, aggregate, and disseminate valuable information as a public good.

This concept was first proposed by Vitalik Buterin in his November 2024 paper, "From Prediction Markets to Information Finance": Future market systems will be meticulously designed to extract specific information from participants—a stark contrast to traditional finance, where information is often accidentally leaked through prices. Its core elements are: **Information Assetization:** Giving value to intangible information, making it measurable and tradable. **AI-Driven Quantification:** Using AI to analyze data from social media, news, etc., transforming ambiguous signals into quantifiable indicators. **Decentralized Distribution:** Using market supply and demand for pricing, replacing platform algorithms, and solving the problems of information opacity and value monopoly in traditional finance.

II. What InfoFi Projects Are There?

1. Kaito: Early Shipment Sparks Controversy

The most attention-grabbing project in this "X ban" is Kaito. Founded in 2022 by Yu Hu, a Cambridge University graduate, Kaito is an AI-driven encrypted search engine and InfoFi network project. Its core is to aggregate massive amounts of fragmented Web3 information through AI technology and realize information assetization, allowing users and creators to profit from their information and attention contributions, thus reconstructing the value distribution system of the Web3 attention economy.

Initially a cryptocurrency-specific search engine, Kaito has since expanded its product line, launching a series of products such as Kaito Pro, Kaito Yaps, and Kaito Connect. Its flagship product, Kaito Pro, utilizes semantic AI technology to index thousands of information sources, including social media, forums, research reports, news, and podcasts, providing a unified and actionable insightful information flow.

In December 2024, Kaito launched Yaps, designed to reward users for sharing valuable insights on X. Yaps is essentially a product where users can earn rewards by posting tweets about brands or projects. While it attracted hundreds of thousands of users and garnered a large following in South Korea, it also contributed to the rise of AI-generated content. Within an hour of Bier's post, Kaito announced it would gradually phase out its "Yaps" product, which rewards users for posting on X, causing a sharp drop in its KAITO (KAITO) token. As of press time, KAITO was trading at $0.5402, a 24-hour drop of 20.6%.

The founder of Kaito posted early this morning:

We will gradually phase out Yaps and the incentivized leaderboard, ushering in a new era for Kaito - Kaito Studio.

Yaps aims to allow users and creators to access content without permission and be rewarded for their role in enhancing brand influence.

Yaps aims to allow users and creators to access content without permission and be rewarded for their role in enhancing brand influence.

Yaps aims to allow users and creators to access content without permission and be rewarded for their role in enhancing brand influence.

Yaps aims to allow users and creators to access content without permission and be rewarded for their role in enhancing brand influence.

It embodies the core principles of Web3—completely free, transparent, and purely merit-based, unrelated to personal connections. Over the past year, we've experimented with stricter eligibility checks, higher leaderboard thresholds, social + on-chain screening mechanisms, and different incentive mechanisms. However, due to platform-wide changes to the X algorithm, and the launch of other InfoFi projects with varying degrees of barriers (some even nonexistent), low-quality and spam issues remain prevalent throughout the cryptocurrency space. For many teams, Kaito has played a crucial role in marketing, branding, and user acquisition. Simultaneously, Kaito has helped hundreds of thousands of new users enter the cryptocurrency space—many of whom are from South Korea, which is now our largest user base country. However, over time, it has become clear that the field has gradually shifted from large-scale, global distribution and airdrop models to more targeted approaches. We learned this directly from various teams and observed the evolution of projects' reward and airdrop mechanisms. Ultimately, the question we had to ask ourselves was not, "Can this be improved further?", but rather, "Is this the right model for the future development of Kaito that we want to build?" After discussions with X, both parties agreed that a completely permissionless distribution system was no longer feasible and did not meet the needs of high-quality brands, serious content creators, or X as a platform. Kaito Studio will be closer to a traditional tiered marketing platform, allowing brands to selectively collaborate with creators based on established criteria and a defined project scope. What's the difference? This means that high-quality creators based on relevance and analytics will benefit more than those using open incentive mechanisms—this will benefit those already using Kaito. This will benefit high-quality creators, as well as those who previously felt disconnected from us. We've been busy developing Kaito Studio for the past few months, so we've been relatively low-key on social media and in announcements, rarely mentioning project development and strategic adjustments. Additionally, I've personally met with some major brands outside the cryptocurrency space to learn from their success stories in traditional industries. The development of Kaito Studio will extend far beyond CT. Currently, the content creator market is worth over $200 billion, and what we know so far is only a small fraction of it. We'll share more details once the studio is ready! I believe this shift is perfectly timed. Cryptocurrency itself is undergoing profound transformation. The vision of an ownership economy has not materialized as many of us anticipated. Meanwhile, the industry's biggest opportunity currently lies in becoming a critical infrastructure for everyday finance—supporting stablecoins, payments, tokenization, privacy, perpetual coins, global capital markets, prediction markets, and many more. In other words, the future of cryptocurrency is not about turning every vertical into a Web3 version, but about deeply exploring the strengths of cryptocurrency. Cryptocurrency will increasingly become the underlying support for many industries that depend on it. We should no longer build and create for cryptocurrency, but for the wider world. Therefore, 2026 will be the year Kaito transcends CT as its primary platform and transcends cryptocurrency as its primary vertical. We hope everyone can join us on this journey. Over the next few days, we will work with all project teams to complete the transition. This change will not affect other Kaito products, including Kaito Pro, Kaito API, Kaito Launchpad, and the upcoming Kaito Markets. Meanwhile, $KAITO will continue to function within Kaito Studio; more details will be announced later. While the post states that "after discussions with X, both parties agreed that a completely permissionless distribution system is no longer feasible and does not meet the needs of high-quality brands, serious content creators, or X as a platform," indicating that the Kaito team had discussed with X in advance and was aware of X's future plans, it still drew considerable skepticism. On January 9th, according to Ember Monitoring, Kaito's multi-signature contract address distributed a total of 24 million $KAITO (approximately $13.31 million USD) to five addresses. One address transferred all 5 million KAITO tokens (approximately $2.82 million) it received to Binance. Crypto KOL "Crypto Fearless" commented: "The Kaito team preemptively sold over $5 million worth of tokens. During their communication with Twitter regarding the API cancellation, the team knew about the negative news and sold off their holdings. Furthermore, the unlocking of Kaito staking has peaked in the past few days. This prior knowledge was also mentioned in Kaito founder YuHu's announcement, stating that he communicated with Twitter." User X commented: "The team perfectly extricated themselves. The retail investors have been tricked by the Kaito project team once again." This feeling is definitely unpleasant, like having your cards exposed beforehand. But it also reminds us that what the team does (on-chain transfers, token unlocking) is more worthy of our close attention than what they say. The data doesn't lie. Goodbye, Web3, this cancer! Additionally, over 1 million KAITO tokens will be unstaking on Friday, 20 to 30 times the usual amount. Since the unstaking period is seven days, some cryptocurrency analysts believe this may indicate that insiders were informed of the ban in advance. vasucrypto points out: $1.1 million worth of KAITO tokens will be unstaking tomorrow, and the KAITO unstaking process takes 7 days. Did they know this would happen beforehand? This is the highest point on the chart, haha. Following the announcement, a large-scale unstaking process began.

2. Cookie DAO (COOKIE): Plunged 12%

Cookie DAO was created by the core team of Cookie3, co-founders of Filip Wielanier (CEO) and Wojciech Piechociński (CTO). It is a decentralized autonomous organization focusing on AI and Web3, with its core positioning as an AI agent index and encrypted data layer. It aggregates on-chain, off-chain, and community data through the cookie.fun platform, using the COOKIE token for governance and incentives, solving the problem of fragmented AI agent data.

Cookie DAO positions itself as the data layer of the smart economy, providing modular data infrastructure for analyzing market dynamics and social interactions. The Cookie ecosystem even supports an autonomous agent called Agent Cookie, which publishes real-time insights on X, and its DataSwarm API also serves projects like CoinGecko. Last May, Cookie DAO launched Cookie Snaps, a platform for projects and creators to trade. Creators can earn Snaps points by referring friends or participating in promotional activities. These points determine their Snap rating, which is used for ranking and rewards. This transparent system allows crypto projects to conduct targeted reward programs while recognizing the role content creators play in increasing project visibility. Following the "X ban," Cookie DAO also announced the shutdown of its similar "Snaps" product, causing its Cookie DAO (COOKIE) token to fall. As of press time, COOKIE is trading at $0.03801, a 24-hour drop of 12.3%.

3.OriginTrail: Underlying Infrastructure

OriginTrail was co-founded by Žiga Drev, Tomaž Levak (CEO), Branimir Rakić (CTO), and others. Project development began in 2013, and the initial issuance of TRAC tokens was completed in January 2018. Its core is a decentralized knowledge graph (DKG) + NeuroWeb blockchain, focusing on trusted data asset integration and AI-driven semantic services. It belongs to the underlying infrastructure project in the InfoFi+AI track.

As the underlying infrastructure of InfoFi, unlike information aggregation projects that focus on the application layer, its core value lies in solving the problems of data credibility and interoperability in the AI ​​era, providing a reliable information foundation for the InfoFi ecosystem. TRAC was least affected, trading at $0.4179 as of press time, a 24-hour drop of 2.3%. III. What do industry insiders think?

  • Dogecoin (DOGE) founder Billy Markus believes that encouraging spam is not a sustainable model.

  • Renowned blockchain detective ZachXBT praised the policy, saying, "Simply put, projects that run low-quality AI ads through the InfoFi platform should be ashamed. Anyone with common sense can see that the fake metrics and unnatural traffic make X virtually unusable for other users."

  • Web3 Collective founder David Choukroun urged X to reconsider, emphasizing that developers like InfoFi seeking revenue streams are not bots.
